critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with many correct instincts. The chronological split, label cutoff discipline, preprocessing fit-only-on-train, and single frozen test evaluation are all appropriate. That said, there are several genuine methodological issues worth examining.

**Issue 1: The labeling scheme conflates proximity-to-work-order with failure imminence, and this conflation may dominate the signal being learned.**

The positive label is defined as: 'an unplanned maintenance work order exists within T_horizon days after the run begins.' This is ambiguous in a critical way. A work order filed on day 13 of a 14-day window after a run begins on day 0 is labeled positive — but if the actual failure (the event triggering the work order) occurred on day 12, the sensor data during the run (days 0–12 before cutoff) may look entirely normal until the last moments. More importantly, the work order filing date may reflect administrative lag, maintenance crew scheduling, or backlog — not physical failure onset. If the plant has systematic patterns in when work orders are filed (e.g., always filed by end-of-shift, always clustered after weekly review meetings, always delayed by parts availability), the model may learn these administrative rhythms rather than physical degradation signals. This is not eliminated by excluding post-work-order data; the problem is that the label generation process injects scheduling artifacts into the positive class definition.

Evidence that would distinguish this: Examine the distribution of (work order date − run start date) for positive cases. If it clusters near T_horizon, the label is measuring 'run happened to start shortly before a scheduled maintenance review,' not 'run started while pump was degrading.' Compare positive label rate across quarters of the calendar year — if it's seasonal, that's an administrative signal. The SHAP/attention analysis in Section 8.3 is necessary but insufficient; you'd want to test a model trained only on calendar features (day of week, time since last scheduled maintenance, maintenance crew capacity) as a third baseline.

**Issue 2: The baseline comparison is structured in a way that confounds temporal modeling with feature richness, despite the stated intent to isolate temporal structure.**

Set A (sequence model) receives raw time-series at 1–5 min intervals, rolling statistics at 1h/8h/24h, rate-of-change features, and maintenance history. Set B (baseline) receives only aggregated summary statistics plus maintenance history. The design says this 'removes the temporal dimension,' but it also removes rate-of-change features and rolling statistics entirely. A logistic regression or XGBoost trained on rolling statistics and rate-of-change features — the same rich feature set as Set A but flattened — is never tested. If the sequence model wins, you cannot determine whether the win came from (a) temporal ordering, (b) the richer derived features that were only given to the sequence model, or (c) the LSTM's capacity. This undermines the core hypothesis test, which is specifically about temporal structure providing signal, not about feature engineering.

Evidence: Add a third baseline — logistic regression or XGBoost on the full Set A features (rolling stats, derivatives, raw sensor summaries at multiple windows) but with all temporal ordering discarded (i.e., concatenate all window statistics into a flat feature vector). If this 'rich flat' baseline matches the LSTM, the hypothesis is not supported. If the LSTM still wins, temporal ordering matters.

**Issue 3: Threshold selection on validation precision/recall may not transfer to the test set under the expected distribution shift.**

The threshold is frozen at 80% recall on the validation set. The validation set is the 0.8–1 year immediately following training. The test set is the 0.8–1 year after that. The design explicitly acknowledges that sensor distributions and failure modes evolve over time. If the positive class rate or the score distributions shift between validation and test (which is likely over a multi-year span in industrial equipment), the 80%-recall threshold calibrated on validation will correspond to a different recall on the test set. Reporting 'precision at the threshold achieving 80% recall on validation' as the primary thresholded test result is therefore measuring precision at an operating point that may be significantly miscalibrated for the test period.

This is not catastrophic — ROC AUC is threshold-free and is the primary metric — but it means the 'operational' results (false-alarm rate, precision at 80% recall) may not reflect deployment reality. Evidence: Report actual recall achieved on the test set at the frozen threshold, not just precision. If recall on test deviates by more than a few points from 80%, the operational metrics are not comparable across the two periods.

**Issue 4: With 3–5 pump units and a chronological split, the stratified-by-pump-model evaluation may be severely underpowered, and the 'hold-out unit' test is almost certainly underpowered.**

At 2–5% positive rate and 3–5 units, the test set contains perhaps 1–2 units worth of data. If one unit has 0 or 1 failure in the test period (very plausible at 2–5% rate), stratified metrics for that unit are meaningless. The proposed confidence intervals are bootstrapped over runs, but with such small positive counts, bootstrap CIs will be wide and the stratified comparison by pump model will have very low statistical power. The 'hold-out site/unit test (if feasible)' in Section 8.4 is especially fragile: training on 2–4 units and testing on 1 unit, with a handful of positive events in the test unit, cannot distinguish 'model generalizes' from 'model happens to predict the 1–2 failures this unit had.'

This is a dataset size constraint that cannot be designed away, but it means the study can at best establish feasibility of the approach, not provide strong evidence for or against the hypothesis at the unit-generalization level. The design should be explicit that pump-model stratification and hold-out unit tests are exploratory, not confirmatory.

Evidence threshold: Count the expected number of positive events per pump model in the test set before running any model. If any stratum has fewer than 10 positive events, stratified performance in that stratum should not be reported as a primary result.

**Issue 5: The attention layer introduces an interpretability confound that the SHAP/attention analysis does not resolve.**

Section 8.3 proposes using attention weights to verify the model relies on early degradation trends. However, it is well-established in the sequence modeling literature that attention weights are not faithful explanations of model decisions — high attention weight does not imply high contribution to the output. Using attention weights to verify what the model 'relies on' is methodologically unsound as a confound control. SHAP on the final feature representation is more defensible, but SHAP on LSTM outputs requires specific implementations (e.g., DeepSHAP or a perturbation-based approach) that can be computationally expensive and may produce approximate attributions. The design should either commit to a specific, validated attribution method or drop the interpretability claim as a confound control and treat it as exploratory only.

This is a genuine flaw because the confound control for 'temporal pattern exploitation' partially relies on this analysis. Without a valid attribution method, the ablation on history length (Issue 8.1) becomes the primary check — which is reasonable but incomplete.

**Summary of verdict reasoning:**
Issues 1 and 2 are the most fundamental. Issue 1 threatens the validity of the labels themselves. Issue 2 means the core comparison does not isolate what the hypothesis claims to test. Issues 3–5 are real but secondary. The design is not fatally broken, but as written it cannot cleanly support or refute the stated hypothesis without addressing issues 1 and 2.
all_issues_raised: The positive label is defined by work-order proximity, which may capture administrative scheduling patterns rather than physical failure onset, injecting systematic confounds into the label generation process.

Set A and Set B differ in both temporal ordering and feature richness (rolling stats, derivatives), so a sequence model win cannot be attributed specifically to temporal structure without a 'rich flat features' baseline.

The threshold frozen at 80% validation recall may correspond to a substantially different recall on the test set if score distributions shift across time periods, making the reported operational metrics unreliable.
With 3–5 pump units at 2–5% positive rate, stratified performance by pump model and hold-out unit tests are likely severely underpowered and cannot distinguish generalization from chance.
Attention weights are not faithful explanations of model decisions, so using them as a confound control for 'temporal pattern exploitation' is methodologically unsound.
Label construction measures horizon from run start rather than from window cutoff, creating positive examples with inconsistent and potentially minimal predictive signal depending on run duration.

The sequence model (Set A) receives richer engineered features including rolling statistics and derivatives while baselines receive only global aggregates (Set B); the critical flattened-Set-A baseline is missing, so observed gains could reflect feature engineering rather than temporal modeling.
3–5 pumps over 4–5 years likely yields fewer than 30 positive events in the test set, making stratified analyses and temporal CV statistically underpowered for the claimed generalization conclusions.
Threshold selection at 80% recall is performed on a small validation positive set (possibly 10–30 events), producing a high-variance, optimistically biased operating point when transferred to test.

The attention layer could exploit temporal position (proximity to window end) as a label-correlated artifact of window construction rather than genuine sensor degradation patterns; a time-shuffle ablation to distinguish positional from content-based attention is absent.
The labeling scheme may introduce soft look-ahead leakage because features from within the T_horizon failure window may be fed to the model, not only features from before the decision point.
The run/label boundary creates systematic mislabeling of near-miss negatives (failures just outside T_horizon), producing asymmetric label noise that inflates apparent negative-class purity.
ROC AUC as the primary metric is optimistic at 2–5% positive prevalence; PR AUC is a necessary co-primary metric to avoid a misleading headline comparison.

Freezing the decision threshold at 80% recall from validation to test is unreliable under the distributional shift expected from a chronological split, and observed test recall may deviate substantially.

The baselines receive a different (smaller) feature set than the sequence model, making it impossible to isolate whether any observed advantage reflects temporal modeling or richer feature engineering.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Overall Assessment

This is a well-structured experimental design with several explicitly correct choices: chronological splitting, preprocessing fit on train only, single test-set evaluation, reasonable confound ablations. However, there are several genuine methodological issues, some of which could silently produce misleading conclusions.

---

### Issue Group 1: Label Construction Creates a Leaky or Ill-Posed Positive Class

**Issue 1: The run-level labeling scheme conflates prediction horizon with run duration in a non-obvious way**

The design labels a run as positive if an unplanned work order exists within T_horizon days *after the run begins*. Consider a run that begins at day 0 and the failure occurs at day 2. That run is labeled positive. But a run that begins at day -30 and the failure occurs at day 2 would *also* be labeled positive (if T_horizon ≥ 2). These two runs have vastly different predictive content — the first has only 2 days of pre-failure signal, the second has 30 days — yet both are treated as equivalent positive examples. The sequence model sees the full 720-hour window regardless, meaning for short-run positives, the signal-rich portion of the window is minimal, while for long-run positives, the model may learn from data arbitrarily far from the actual failure.

The labeling rule as stated does *not* ensure that the 720-hour window before the prediction cutoff consistently contains pre-failure degradation signals. For a run beginning at day 0 with failure at day 1, the model's 720-hour window extends 30 days into the past — before the run even started — and the predictive content within the run itself is sparse.

The correct label construction should probably be: a run is positive if the *end* of the data window (or equivalently a fixed prediction horizon from 'now') falls within T_horizon days of the failure. As written, it is unclear whether the horizon is measured from run start or from the window cutoff, and the design nowhere addresses how run duration interacts with the labeling window.

Evidence against this: If the design implicitly assumes run duration >> T_horizon (i.e., all runs are long), this is harmless. Evidence for this: Distribution of run durations in the data; if any runs are shorter than T_horizon, the labeling is definitionally confused.

---

### Issue Group 2: The Baseline Comparison Does Not Isolate the Claimed Advantage

**Issue 2: The sequence model receives Set A (which includes rolling statistics and derivative features), while the baseline receives Set B (only raw aggregates). These are not equivalent feature budgets.**

Set A includes 1h/8h/24h rolling means, stds, and higher-order moments *per sensor*, plus velocity and acceleration features. Set B includes only global summary statistics over the full window. The sequence model therefore receives far richer features — rolling statistics are already a partial compression of temporal structure. If the LSTM trained on Set A outperforms logistic regression on Set B, this does not isolate *temporal modeling* as the source of gain; it could simply reflect the richer feature engineering in Set A.

A fair test would be: logistic regression (or XGBoost) trained on Set A features flattened. This would isolate whether the LSTM's inductive bias (temporal dependencies) contributes over and above having access to the same temporal summary features. The design partially addresses this with XGBoost on Set B, but XGBoost also receives only Set B, so the confound persists.

Evidence: Train logistic regression on Set A (flattened). If it matches or exceeds the LSTM, the advantage is feature engineering, not the sequence model. This baseline is missing.

**Issue 3: Threshold selection on validation data introduces asymmetric optimism for the sequence model**

The threshold achieving 80% recall is selected on the validation set for the sequence model, then precision is reported on the test set. The baselines presumably receive the same treatment. However, with a 2–5% positive rate and a relatively small number of failure events (3–5 pumps over 4–5 years yields perhaps 50–150 failure events total, of which ~20% are in validation), the recall-at-80% threshold is estimated from perhaps 10–30 positive validation examples. This threshold estimate has high variance and will be optimistically biased when applied to the test set. Bootstrap confidence intervals on test metrics address variance but not the bias from threshold selection.

Evidence: Report precision-recall curves on test rather than single operating point, or report calibration of the threshold transfer from validation to test.

---

### Issue Group 3: Statistical Power and Generalization Are Inadequate for the Stated Claims

**Issue 4: 3–5 pumps over 4–5 years yields too few failure events for the claims being made, especially for stratified analysis**

At a 2–5% positive rate among runs, the total number of failure events depends on the number of distinct runs. If a pump runs continuously with periodic restarts, there may be very few "runs" — perhaps 20–100 per pump per year, giving 200–500 runs total and 10–25 positive events in the test set. This is far too few to (a) estimate ROC AUC with tight confidence intervals stratified by pump model, (b) perform meaningful temporal cross-validation with 5 folds, or (c) detect distribution shift across time periods. The 95% bootstrap confidence intervals are likely to be wide enough to preclude any definitive conclusion.

This is not a generic "more data is better" complaint — it is a structural issue: the design's stratified analyses and CV procedures assume enough events per stratum to yield stable estimates, but the data regime described may provide only single-digit failure events per pump model in the test set.

Evidence: Count the expected number of positive test-set events given the described data. If the lower bound is fewer than ~30 across all strata, the stratified analyses are ungeneralizable and the confidence intervals will overlap across all comparisons.

---

### Issue Group 4: Silent Misconfiguration Risks

**Issue 5: The attention mechanism could exploit the temporal position of the window end as a proxy for nearness to failure**

The window for each example is defined as the 720 hours before the prediction horizon cutoff. For positive examples, the cutoff is close to an actual failure (within T_horizon days). For negative examples, the cutoff is arbitrary. The attention layer may learn to weight recency (end of window) more heavily for positives simply because recent sensor readings are more likely to reflect degradation, which is legitimate — but it could also learn positional cues that are artifacts of how windows were constructed (e.g., if windows for positives are systematically truncated at a different point in the sensor cycle than negatives). This is a subtle form of label leakage via temporal context.

The ablation on history length (Issue 8 in the design) partially addresses this, but does not specifically check whether attention weights correlate with absolute temporal position rather than with sensor anomaly patterns. Attention weight analysis should visualize position-weighted vs. content-weighted attention.

Evidence: Shuffle the time steps within each window (destroying temporal order) and retrain. If performance drops substantially, temporal structure is being used. If it barely drops, the model is mostly using positional cues or summary statistics, not sequential patterns — which would undermine the hypothesis.

---

### Summary of Genuine Issues

1. **Label construction ambiguity**: The horizon measured from run start vs. window cutoff creates a positive class with inconsistent predictive content across examples. This could silently inflate or deflate performance depending on run duration distribution.

2. **Unfair baseline feature sets**: The sequence model receives richer engineered features (Set A) while baselines receive only raw aggregates (Set B). A flattened-Set-A baseline is missing, so any observed advantage could be feature engineering, not temporal modeling.

3. **Low event count undermines stratified claims**: With 3–5 pumps, the stratified analyses and temporal CV are likely underpowered, making the core generalization claims statistically unsupported.

4. **Threshold transfer variance**: Selecting the operating threshold from a small validation positive set introduces optimistic bias in single-operating-point test metrics.

5. **Attention positional confound**: The attention layer may use positional cues correlated with label construction rather than genuine degradation patterns; a time-shuffle ablation is missing.

issues_raised: The labeling scheme may introduce soft look-ahead leakage because features from within the T_horizon failure window may be fed to the model, not only features from before the decision point.
The run/label boundary creates systematic mislabeling of near-miss negatives (failures just outside T_horizon), producing asymmetric label noise that inflates apparent negative-class purity.
ROC AUC as the primary metric is optimistic at 2–5% positive prevalence; PR AUC is a necessary co-primary metric to avoid a misleading headline comparison.

Freezing the decision threshold at 80% recall from validation to test is unreliable under the distributional shift expected from a chronological split, and observed test recall may deviate substantially.

The baselines receive a different (smaller) feature set than the sequence model, making it impossible to isolate whether any observed advantage reflects temporal modeling or richer feature engineering.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with genuine attention to temporal leakage, chronological splitting, and evaluation hygiene. The critique below focuses on real structural issues, not nits.

**Issue 1: The labeling scheme conflates run-start with the prediction point, creating a variable and potentially unlimited look-ahead window**

The specific claim: Labeling a run as 'failure imminent' if an unplanned work order occurs within T_horizon days *after the run begins* creates a realistic early-detection label.

Why this might be wrong: A pump run may last 30, 90, or 200 hours. The 'prediction point' is implicitly the start of the run, but the window T_horizon (7–14 days) begins there too. If a run starts, then 10 days later a failure occurs, the model is trained to flag the run from its *start* — but the model is fed sensor data from the 720-hour (30-day) history preceding... what exactly? The design says 'before the prediction horizon cutoff' but the prediction horizon cutoff is not the run start; it's T_horizon days after run start. So for a positive run, the model may actually receive up to T_horizon days of data *from within the failure window itself*. The exclusion criterion ('exclude data after the work-order date') does not help here because the work-order date could be 8–14 days into the labeled window. The model therefore may see early-degradation sensor readings that are inside the labeled positive period, not before it. This is a soft form of look-ahead: the model learns from degradation signals that occur *after* the decision point would have needed to be made in production.

What would constitute evidence: Restate exactly when the prediction point is defined per run (e.g., always the moment T_horizon before the work order), extract features from only before that point, and show that performance is stable across runs of different lengths. If AUC drops significantly when a strict per-run cutoff is applied, the original leakage was providing signal.

**Issue 2: The run-level positive/negative label definition is ambiguous for long runs crossing the failure window multiple times**

The specific claim: Each pump run is a coherent unit that can be cleanly labeled as positive or negative.

Why this might be wrong: The design does not define what constitutes a 'run.' If a pump operates continuously for 60 days, is that one run? If so, it might be labeled negative even though it has slow degradation throughout. If runs are split at arbitrary intervals, that creates artificial boundaries. More problematically, a run labeled negative because its unplanned maintenance occurs at day 15 (just outside a T_horizon=14 day window) is conceptually identical to a positive run — the pump was failing; the label flips based on a 1-day timing difference. This label noise is asymmetric: it systematically mislabels near-miss negative cases as true negatives, which suppresses measured precision and inflates the apparent difficulty of the problem.

What would constitute evidence: Plot the distribution of time-to-next-failure for 'negative' labeled runs. If a substantial fraction have unplanned maintenance at day 15–30 (just outside the horizon), the label boundary is creating systematic noise. A sensitivity analysis varying T_horizon by ±3 days and measuring AUC stability would quantify this effect.

**Issue 3: ROC AUC as primary metric is inappropriate for 2–5% positive prevalence when the sequence vs. baseline comparison is the core claim**

The specific claim: ROC AUC 'emphasizes consistent ranking across strata rather than tying the headline result to one prevalence regime.'

Why this might be wrong: At 2–5% positive rate, ROC AUC is well-known to be optimistic because it averages over a large number of high-threshold operating points where the model trivially assigns low scores to negatives. A sequence model with richer features may learn a smoother score distribution that looks better under AUC without being meaningfully better at the operating points that matter. The design does report precision at the 80% recall threshold, which is good, but PR AUC (or average precision) is the metric that integrates over operating points in a way that is sensitive to rare-class performance. If the sequence model's advantage is concentrated in the ROC AUC but not in PR AUC or precision at fixed recall, that would indicate the benefit is in low-stakes operating regions.

What would constitute evidence: Report PR AUC (average precision) alongside ROC AUC as a co-primary metric. If the gap between sequence model and logistic regression is substantially smaller in PR AUC than ROC AUC, the headline result is misleading.

**Issue 4: The threshold chosen on validation (80% recall) is transferred to test without accounting for the well-known train-val recall instability under class imbalance**

The specific claim: A threshold frozen on validation data at 80% recall will produce approximately 80% recall and interpretable precision on the test set.

Why this might be wrong: At 2–5% positive rate, a small shift in the score distribution between validation and test (plausible given chronological split and time-evolving failure modes) will cause the frozen threshold to produce substantially different recall on test. This is not a minor calibration issue — recall can swing 10–20 percentage points under modest distributional shift at low prevalence. The design acknowledges temporal evolution as a risk but does not address that the fixed threshold will silently misrepresent operational performance on the test set.

What would constitute evidence: Report the actual recall achieved on the test set at the frozen threshold, alongside the targeted 80%. If observed recall deviates by more than 5 percentage points, the fixed-threshold reporting is misleading and threshold-free metrics (PR AUC) should be primary.

**Issue 5: The 'same tuning effort' claim for the baselines is not operationally equivalent to LSTM tuning effort**

The specific claim: Logistic regression and XGBoost receive 'identical tuning effort' to the sequence model, ensuring a fair comparison.

Why this might be wrong: Grid search over regularization strength for logistic regression has a fundamentally smaller search space than the LSTM grid (learning rate × units × dropout × batch size × architecture depth). More importantly, the LSTM receives a richer feature set (Set A) while the baselines receive Set B. The design conflates 'same tuning effort' with 'same tuning protocol,' which is not the same thing. A fairer comparison would be: (a) train the baselines on Set A (same features, different model) and (b) train the LSTM on Set B (same features, different model) as cross-conditions. Without these cross-conditions, it is impossible to separate the contribution of temporal features from the contribution of temporal modeling.

What would constitute evidence: Add a logistic regression on Set A (i.e., all rolling stats and rate-of-change features flattened into a wide feature vector). If this matches the LSTM, the advantage is in feature engineering, not sequence modeling. If the LSTM still wins with both trained on Set A, the architecture contributes. This is the most important missing baseline for the core hypothesis.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 5 are the most fundamental. Issue 1 potentially introduces soft look-ahead leakage from the labeling scheme. Issue 5 means the core hypothesis (temporal structure provides signal, not just richer features) cannot be tested as currently designed because the models receive different feature sets by construction. The remaining issues are real but would not individually invalidate the experiment.
